flashcards.front: What are the four layers of the TCP/IP model?
flashcards.back: 1. Application Layer 2. Transport Layer 3. Internet Layer 4. Network Access Layer (also called Link Layer). These correspond to OSI model's 7 layers.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?
flashcards.back: TCP is connection-oriented, reliable, provides error correction and flow control. UDP is connectionless, unreliable, faster but no guarantee of delivery or order.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of IP (Internet Protocol)?
flashcards.back: IP provides logical addressing and routing of packets across interconnected networks. It handles logical addressing (IP addresses) and determines the best path for data delivery.

flashcards.front: What is ARP and what does it do?
flashcards.back: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) maps IP addresses to MAC (hardware) addresses on a local network. It resolves known IP addresses to unknown MAC addresses.

flashcards.front: What is the three-way handshake in TCP?
flashcards.back: 1. SYN: Client sends SYN segment 2. SYN-ACK: Server responds with SYN-ACK 3. ACK: Client confirms with ACK. This establishes a reliable connection.

flashcards.front: What is DHCP and how does it work?
flashcards.back: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) automatically assigns IP addresses to network devices. It uses DORA process: Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge.

flashcards.front: What is DNS and what is its purpose?
flashcards.back: DNS (Domain Name System) translates domain names (like google.com) to IP addresses. It acts as a distributed database of domain name to IP address mappings.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between public and private IP addresses?
flashcards.back: Public IP addresses are unique globally and used for internet communication. Private IPs (10.x.x.x, 172.16-31.x.x, 192.168.x.x) are used within local networks and cannot be routed on the internet.

flashcards.front: What is subnet mask and its purpose?
flashcards.back: A subnet mask is a 32-bit number that divides an IP address into network and host portions. It determines which part of the IP address identifies the network and which identifies the host device.

flashcards.front: What is ICMP and when is it used?
flashcards.back: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) is used for error reporting and diagnostics. Tools like ping and traceroute use ICMP to test connectivity and diagnose network issues.

flashcards.front: What are well-known port numbers? Give examples.
flashcards.back: Well-known ports range from 0-1023. Examples: HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, FTP=21, SMTP=25, DNS=53, SSH=22, Telnet=23.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between TCP/IP and OSI model?
flashcards.back: TCP/IP has 4 layers, OSI has 7. TCP/IP is the actual protocol suite used on the internet; OSI is a conceptual reference model. TCP/IP combines some OSI layers (Application, Presentation, Session into one).

flashcards.front: What is RARP and how does it differ from ARP?
flashcards.back: RARP (Reverse ARP) maps MAC addresses to IP addresses (opposite of ARP). Used by diskless workstations to obtain IP addresses. Largely replaced by BOOTP and DHCP.
The default gateway is the router interface that connects a local network to other networks (typically the internet). It routes traffic outside the local network.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between connection-oriented and connectionless protocols?
flashcards.back: Connection-oriented (TCP) establishes a virtual connection before sending data and ensures reliable delivery. Connectionless (UDP) sends data without establishing connection, no reliability guarantee.
